title: Higher Dimensional Birkhoff Attractors
speaker: Vincent Humilière
abstract: The Birkhoff attractor is a closed invariant subset associated with any dissipative twist map of the annulus (of dimension 2), which was introduced by Birkhoff in 1932. We will see that it can be generalized to higher dimensions using tools from symplectic topology, namely the spectral distance and the gamma-support. This is based on joint work with Marie-Claude Arnaud and Claude Viterbo.

proof_stages.label: Classical annular Birkhoff attractor and the generalization problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Birkhoff's annular construction for a dissipative map, distinguishes the standard intersection attractor from the hair-cut Birkhoff attractor, and formulates the difficulty of finding an upper/lower replacement in higher dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Conformally symplectic framework and Lagrangian heuristic
proof_stages.summary: Conformally exact symplectic maps on cotangent bundles are chosen as the higher-dimensional setting, with damped Hamiltonian and Liouville-flow models motivating the expectation that the attractor should be a generalized Lagrangian object.

proof_stages.label: Spectral norm completion and fixed point
proof_stages.summary: The Viterbo spectral norm gamma on exact Lagrangians is recalled, conformally exact symplectic maps are shown to act by gamma-contractions, and the completion therefore contains a unique fixed point N_infinity(psi).

proof_stages.label: Gamma support and its structural properties
proof_stages.summary: The gamma support assigns a closed subset of the cotangent bundle to an element of the gamma-completion and is shown or recalled to have the key properties needed later: invariance, fiber-intersection, semicontinuity, generalized coisotropicity, homological largeness, connectedness, and a sheaf-theoretic interpretation as reduced microsupport.

proof_stages.label: Generalized Birkhoff attractor
proof_stages.summary: The higher-dimensional Birkhoff attractor is defined as the gamma support of the unique fixed point N_infinity(psi), inherits the gamma-support properties, recovers the classical object on T*S^1, and is discussed in Liouville/Weinstein settings.

proof_stages.label: Completed-Hamiltonian conjugacy interlude
proof_stages.summary: A fixed-point argument on the gamma-completion of the Hamiltonian group shows that conformally symplectic maps with the same ratio and Hamiltonian-related perturbations are conjugate in the completion, yielding a weak nearby-Lagrangian statement.

proof_stages.label: Agreement with the classical annular attractor
proof_stages.summary: Using the classical attractor's minimality, horizontal Lagrangian circles corrected by vertical translations, gamma-convergence to the fixed point, and semicontinuity of gamma support, the speaker proves the generalized attractor is contained in the classical lambda(psi), hence coincides with it in dimension two.

proof_stages.label: Discounted Hamilton-Jacobi connection and vanishing friction
proof_stages.summary: The talk relates the generalized Birkhoff attractor to discounted Hamilton-Jacobi equations by showing that differentials of Tonelli viscosity solutions lie in it, then contrasts this with the failure of gamma-convergence in a vanishing-friction limit.

examples.name: Damped Hamiltonian flow with friction
examples.note: A Hamiltonian system with an added friction/Liouville term is used to make conformally symplectic contraction concrete, including the spiral-looking two-dimensional attractor as friction varies.

examples.name: Liouville-flow convergence to the zero section
examples.note: The standard Liouville flow sends all points of a cotangent bundle to the zero section, and this observation is explicitly used to motivate why the higher-dimensional attractor should be a generalized Lagrangian.

examples.name: Weinstein skeleton comparison
examples.note: In response to a question about the naive intersection attractor, the Weinstein/Liouville skeleton is used to illustrate that the generalized Birkhoff attractor should retain only suitable Lagrangian/coisotropic parts.
examples.engagement_reason: in the Weinstein skeleton comparison the speaker analyzes how the naive attractor gives the full skeleton while the generalized attractor retains only appropriate Lagrangian/coisotropic parts

passes.reason: the Weinstein skeleton comparison shows the naive full skeleton cannot generally be the coisotropic Birkhoff attractor
